summary: Gruber examines Bill Gates's admission that losing mobile to Android was his 'greatest mistake ever,' focusing on the irony that Gates frames the opportunity as being 'the standard non-Apple phone platform' — implicitly conceding the iPhone's inevitability. Gruber traces a provocative thread back to 1997, when Microsoft's commitment to Office for Mac helped save Apple from near-death. He argues that Microsoft Office singlehandedly kept the Mac credible for eight years until Apple's own iWork suite arrived. The piece builds to a paradoxical conclusion: if Gates's biggest mistake was losing mobile to Android, the seeds were planted when Microsoft chose to support the very company that would later create the iPhone.
key_points: Gates's framing assumes the iPhone was inevitable and the only opportunity was to be the dominant non-Apple platform — a remarkable concession from someone who once dominated the industry
Microsoft's 1997 investment in Apple was less about the $150 million and more about the commitment to continue developing Office for Mac, which kept the Mac platform viable for nearly a decade
Without Office for Mac, Apple may not have survived to create the iPhone, and without the iPhone, the modern smartphone market might never have emerged
The antitrust pressure Microsoft faced in the 1990s may have influenced their decision to support Apple rather than let it wither
Gates's 'winner take all' framing of platforms inadvertently makes the case for regulating platform companies
money_quotes: It's just assumed in his thinking that the iPhone would have been the iPhone no matter what. Historically, that sounds bananas coming out of Bill Gates's mouth.
The value for Apple wasn't the money itself but the public show of confidence from Microsoft — the message that Microsoft was supporting Apple, not trying to crush them.
Microsoft Office singlehandedly kept the Mac a credible platform for classic productivity apps for 8 years.
With no Office 98, there might not have been an Apple to even make the iPhone in 2007.

Without the iPhone, I think there's a chance the mobile market would have continued on the same course it was on before the iPhone: dominated by crap software and BlackBerry-style hardware, with the carriers calling the shots.
If losing the mobile market to Android was Gates's biggest mistake, you can argue it started when he agreed to support Apple and the Mac in 1997.
key_insight: Gates's greatest mistake may trace back to saving Apple in 1997 — the very act that enabled the company to later create the iPhone and reshape the mobile market Microsoft failed to win.
